examples.title: Algorithm: Programmed I/O with Polling
examples.description: Demonstrates programmed I/O where CPU continuously polls device status register until device is ready
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ProgrammedIOPoll
INPUT: Device address, data to write
OUTPUT: Data written to device

BEGIN
 // Define device register addresses
 STATUS_REG ← 0x1000 // Device status register address
 DATA_REG ← 0x1004 // Device data register address
 READY_BIT ← 0x01 // Bit mask for ready flag

 FUNCTION WriteToDevice(data)
 WHILE TRUE DO
 // Read status register
 status ← READ(STATUS_REG)
 
 // Check if device is ready
 IF (status AND READY_BIT) ≠ 0 THEN
 // Device is ready, break out of loop
 BREAK
 END IF
 // Continue polling - CPU is busy waiting
 END WHILE
 
 // Write data to device data register
 WRITE(DATA_REG, data)
 
 RETURN success
 END FUNCTION

 // Example: Write character to printer
 FUNCTION PrintCharacter(char)
 // Wait for printer to be ready
 WHILE (READ(STATUS_REG) AND 0x01) = 0 DO
 // Busy wait - CPU wastes cycles
 NULL
 END WHILE
 
 // Write character to printer data register
 WRITE(DATA_REG, char)
 END FUNCTION

 // Example: Read data from input device
 FUNCTION ReadFromDevice()
 // Wait for data to be available
 WHILE (READ(STATUS_REG) AND 0x02) = 0 DO
 NULL
 END WHILE
 
 // Read data from device
 data ← READ(DATA_REG)
 RETURN data
 END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates Programmed I/O (Polling). The CPU continuously reads the status register in a loop until the device sets the ready bit. This is simple but inefficient as the CPU wastes cycles busy-waiting. The STATUS_REG is polled to check if device is ready (READY_BIT set), then DATA_REG is used for actual data transfer.

examples.title: Python: Memory-Mapped I/O Simulation
examples.description: Simulates memory-mapped I/O device access using Python classes with hardware register semantics
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Memory-Mapped I/O Simulation
Demonstrates how memory-mapped I/O works by simulating device registers
mapped to specific memory addresses.
"""

import time

class MemoryMappedIO:
 """
 Simulates memory-mapped I/O where device registers appear at specific
 memory addresses, just like real hardware.
 """
 
 def __init__(self):
 # Simulated memory space (0x0000 to 0xFFFF)
 self.memory = bytearray(65536)
 
 # Device 1: Serial Port Registers (mapped at 0x1000)
 self.DEV1_STATUS = 0x1000 # Status register
 self.DEV1_DATA = 0x1004 # Data register
 self.DEV1_CONTROL = 0x1008 # Control register
 
 # Status bit definitions
 self.STATUS_RX_READY = 0x01 # Receive data ready
 self.STATUS_TX_READY = 0x02 # Transmit ready
 self.STATUS_ERROR = 0x80 # Error flag
 
 # Initialize device buffers
 self.tx_buffer = []
 self.rx_buffer = []
 
 def read_register(self, address):
 """Read from a memory-mapped register (32-bit integer)"""
 # In real hardware, this would be a hardware read
 # Simulate by computing status based on buffer state
 if address == self.DEV1_STATUS:
 status = 0
 if len(self.rx_buffer) > 0:
 status |= self.STATUS_RX_READY
 if len(self.tx_buffer) < 8: # Can accept more data
 status |= self.STATUS_TX_READY
 return status
 elif address == self.DEV1_DATA:
 if self.rx_buffer:
 return ord(self.rx_buffer.pop(0))
 return 0
 elif address == self.DEV1_CONTROL:
 return 0x01 # Device enabled
 return 0
 
 def write_register(self, address, value):
 """Write to a memory-mapped register (32-bit integer)"""
 if address == self.DEV1_STATUS:
 pass # Read-only in this simulation
 elif address == self.DEV1_DATA:
 # Write character to transmit buffer
 char = chr(value & 0xFF)
 self.tx_buffer.append(char)
 print(f"[UART] Transmitted: '{char}'")
 elif address == self.DEV1_CONTROL:
 print(f"[UART] Control register written: 0x{value:02x}")
 
 def simulate_rx_data(self, data):
 """Simulate incoming data to the device"""
 self.rx_buffer.extend(list(data))
 
 def memory_read(self, address):
 """Generic memory read - for non-device addresses, return 0"""
 if address >= 0x1000 and address < 0x2000:
 return self.read_register(address)
 return 0
 
 def memory_write(self, address, value):
 """Generic memory write"""
 if address >= 0x1000 and address < 0x2000:
 self.write_register(address, value)


class IODevice:
 """I/O Device controller with memory-mapped interface"""
 
 def __init__(self, io_system):
 self.io = io_system
 self.STATUS = io_system.DEV1_STATUS
 self.DATA = io_system.DEV1_DATA
 
 def poll_for_ready(self):
 """Programmed I/O: Poll status register until ready"""
 while True:
 status = self.io.memory_read(self.STATUS)
 if status & io_system.STATUS_TX_READY:
 return True
 # In real hardware, CPU would be busy waiting here
 time.sleep(0.01)
 
 def write_char(self, char):
 """Write a character using memory-mapped I/O"""
 # Poll until device is ready
 self.poll_for_ready()
 
 # Write data to device (memory-mapped address)
 self.io.memory_write(self.DATA, ord(char))
 
 return True
 
 def read_char(self):
 """Read a character using memory-mapped I/O"""
 # Poll for received data
 while True:
 status = self.io.memory_read(self.STATUS)
 if status & self.io.STATUS_RX_READY:
 break
 time.sleep(0.01)
 
 # Read from device data register
 data = self.io.memory_read(self.DATA)
 return chr(data)


def main():
 """Demonstrate memory-mapped I/O operations"""
 print("=" * 50)
 print("Memory-Mapped I/O Simulation")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 # Create I/O system
 io_system = MemoryMappedIO()
 device = IODevice(io_system)
 
 # Simulate incoming data
 io_system.simulate_rx_data("Hello, World!")
 
 # Demonstrate programmed I/O (polling) for output
 print("\n--- Transmitting Data (Polling) ---")
 message = "Hi"
 for char in message:
 device.write_char(char)
 print(f"CPU wrote '{char}' to address 0x{device.DATA:04X}")
 
 # Demonstrate reading using polling
 print("\n--- Receiving Data (Polling) ---")
 while True:
 status = io_system.memory_read(io_system.DEV1_STATUS)
 if status & io_system.STATUS_RX_READY:
 char = device.read_char()
 print(f"CPU read '{char}' from address 0x{device.DATA:04X}")
 else:
 break
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 50)
 print("Memory-Mapped I/O Demo Complete")
 print("=" * 50)


if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates memory-mapped I/O where device registers appear at specific memory addresses (0x1000-0x1008). The IODevice class shows programmed I/O (polling) where the CPU continuously checks the status register until the device is ready. Key concepts: device registers are accessed like normal memory locations, STATUS register contains flags (RX_READY, TX_READY), and DATA register holds the actual data being transferred.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of I/O operations, polling adds overhead
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for registers, O(n) for buffers

examples.title: C: Interrupt-Driven I/O Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates interrupt-driven I/O with ISR handler, showing how CPU handles device interrupts instead of polling
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates interrupt-driven I/O using a UART (serial) device as an example. Key concepts: (1) Memory-mapped registers accessed via volatile pointers, (2) ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) is triggered by hardware instead of CPU polling, (3) Circular buffers decouple CPU from I/O device, (4) UART_IMSC controls which interrupts are enabled, (5) Non-blocking API allows CPU to do other work while I/O happens asynchronously. This is far more efficient than programmed I/O as the CPU isn't busy-wasting.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for data transfer, O(1) for CPU time per interrupt

examples.title: C: DMA Transfer Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates Direct Memory Access controller setup for efficient block data transfers without CPU intervention
examples.explanation: This C example demonstrates Direct Memory Access (DMA) which allows block data transfer without CPU intervention. Key elements: (1) DMA registers (SAR, DAR, CCR, CNDTR) control transfer parameters, (2) CPU programs DMA with source/destination addresses and byte count, (3) DMA controller handles the actual data transfer, (4) CPU is free to perform other tasks while DMA transfers, (5) Interrupt signals completion. Three modes are explained: Burst (fastest, CPU blocked), Cycle Stealing (balanced), and Transparent (slowest, no CPU impact).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for transfer, O(1) CPU time for setup
